g.data-narration: Welcome to Static Class Members in C++. This powerful feature bridges OOP and procedural programming for university exam success.
g.text: BCS306B - Object Oriented Programming with C++

g.data-narration: Static members belong to the class itself, not individual objects. One copy is shared by all objects, unlike regular instance members.
g.text: Unlike regular members (each object has its own copy),
static members exist as ONE shared copy for the entire class.
They belong to the class, not to any specific object.
Object A | Object B | Object C

g.data-narration: Static members enable object counting, shared configuration data, and singleton patterns - essential for memory-efficient C++ programs.
g.text: • Count objects created across all instances

g.data-narration: Static data members are declared inside the class using the static keyword. This declares them as class variables, not instance variables.
g.text: Declared inside class using static keyword:
✓ Shared by ALL objects of the class
✓ Exists even with zero objects created

g.data-narration: Static members MUST be defined outside the class with the scope resolution operator. This allocates storage in the data segment.
g.text: ⚠ MUST define outside class - declaration alone causes linker error!
// Outside class definition, typically in .cpp file
const static members can initialize inside class:

g.data-narration: Static member functions belong to the class, not objects. They can be called without creating any object using ClassName::function().
g.text: Functions that belong to the class, callable without objects:

g.data-narration: Critical concept: Static functions have NO 'this' pointer and cannot access non-static members directly. This is a common exam question!
g.text: Static functions CANNOT access non-static (instance) members!
✓ Can be called without creating objects

g.data-narration: Static members can be accessed two ways: using ClassName::member (class scope) or object.member (object scope). Both are valid.
g.text: Two ways to access static members:

g.data-narration: This example counts objects created using a static variable incremented in the constructor. The static getter returns total count.
BankAccount shows shared interest rate - when changed via static setter, it affects ALL accounts instantly. Perfect for global config.
g.text: static void setRate(double r) { interestRate = r; }

g.data-narration: In inheritance, static members are NOT overridden - they are shared. Derived class static with same name HIDES base static member.
g.text: Static members are NOT overridden - they are shared across base and derived classes.
class Derived : public Base { static int value; } // Hides Base::value

g.data-narration: Key differences: static has one copy shared by all objects, exists without objects, not in sizeof. Non-static has per-object copies.
Avoid these exam pitfalls: forgetting external definition, trying to access non-static from static, initializing in constructor.
Remember: declaration uses static keyword inside class, definition uses :: outside. Access via ClassName::member or object.member.
university exam tips: static members aren't in sizeof, can't be virtual, can only access other statics directly. Practice previous year questions!
g.text: • Static members NOT in sizeof(class) • Static can't be virtual • Static can't use 'this'
• Remember external definition syntax • Review previous university question papers for patterns
